revised_position: SSE is the correct default for unidirectional notification workloads, but I'm less categorical than Elena on the margins. Her architectural argument is sound — the protocol should match the data flow. Where I diverge is the claim that SSE's complexity is 'free.' You still need server-side connection registry, channel subscription management, auth token rotation for long-lived connections, and monitoring for silent connection drops. EventSource's built-in reconnection is basic primitive, not a production-grade solution — most teams end up wrapping it with custom backoff anyway. Last-Event-ID gives you at-least-once resumption, not exactly-once as implied; duplicate delivery on reconnection is a real protocol edge case that matters for financial notification workloads. And the HTTP/2 multiplexing story gets constrained at scale — browsers enforce per-domain connection limits (typically 6 EventSources per origin in Chrome) that bite you if you architect many distinct notification channels via separate SSE streams. I'd keep one SSE connection per origin and multiplex channels within it via a lightweight event routing scheme. Even then: at very high connection counts (50K+), the per-connection overhead of SSE (even with HTTP/2) starts to meaningfully differ from WebSocket's single-upgrade, binary-framing economy. The polyglot strategy I advocate — SSE first, measure, upgrade specific channels to WebSocket only when measured latency or throughput requirements exceed what SSE delivers — stands. Elena is right on the default direction but understates the operational surface of SSE at real scale.

conceded_to.point: SSE rides on existing HTTP infrastructure — load balancers, CDNs, and proxies handle it natively without per-hop upgrade configuration. The operational simplicity this buys is real and I've underweighted it in my own assessments.

The unidirectional framing overhead argument is correct — if you're not using the return channel, WebSocket is paying a framing tax for bidirectional capability you don't consume. SSE's EventStream framing maps directly to the notification data flow.

WebSocket's lack of standardized reconnection is a genuine liability. The 'implement it yourself' tax is visible in production — I've seen the reconnect-storm failure mode at scale and it's devastating.

disagrees_with.point: Characterizes Last-Event-ID resumption as 'exactly-once' when it's at-least-once with a deterministic replay point. The server may have already sent events 103-107 before the drop; the client reconnects with Last-Event-ID: 102 and receives 103-107 again. For order-fill notifications and financial workloads, duplicate delivery is a correctness issue, not a cosmetic one.

Claims SSE's reliability comes 'for free from the HTTP stack,' which understates the server-side surface area. You still need: connection registry (which clients on which channels), key rotation for SSE endpoints that live longer than a token lifetime, health-check infrastructure that distinguishes 'connected but idle' from 'connected and processing,' and reconnection state management that's barely simpler than WebSocket's when you need custom backoff or channel-specific resubscription.

HTTP/2 multiplexing for SSE has practical limits — browsers enforce per-origin connection caps (typically 6 concurrent EventSources in Chrome). If you architect separate SSE connections per notification channel type, you hit this ceiling quickly. The right mitigation is channel multiplexing within a single EventSource (one connection, routed by event type), which adds protocol complexity back.

new_insights: The deciding variable I see at scale is rarely protocol capability — it's team expertise with the chosen protocol's failure modes. I've seen SSE fail because a team didn't know HTTP/2 connection limits existed. I've seen WebSocket succeed because a team had already built the reconnection infrastructure for a prior project. The 'which protocol' question is secondary to 'which failure modes does your team know how to debug at 3AM.'

There's an architectural pattern I've used successfully that sits between SSE and WebSocket: a single SSE connection per origin with application-level event routing (event: channel.name fields), paired with a separate HTTP POST endpoint for client-to-server subscription/unsubscription messages. This gets you most of WebSocket's flexibility with SSE's infrastructure simplicity, and each notification channel can be independently upgraded to WebSocket when measured data shows SSE's performance is the bottleneck. The upgrade path is per-channel, not all-or-nothing.

The protocol decision is path-dependent in a way Elena's framing doesn't capture. If you're in an environment that already has WebSocket infrastructure (proxies configured, monitoring in place, reconnection library written), the incremental cost of adding another WebSocket endpoint is near zero — and choosing SSE instead adds a new transport to maintain. The 'SSE first' rule makes most sense for greenfield systems and loses force as WebSocket operational maturity increases.
